hub_subtitle: Conversação para viagens, listening de verdade e reuniões internacionais — rumo a Nova York, setembro de 2026

lesson.menu_title: Getting Around Like a Local -- Airports, Transit, and the Rules of the City
lesson.menu_desc: Do quiosque de check-in em Guarulhos a catraca em Manhattan: have to, don't have to, can't e be allowed to -- a gramatica de uma regra -- 39 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 6 -- Getting Around Like a Local
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Lucimara | Aula 6 -- Getting Around Like a Local

listenings.text: Ladies and gentlemen, may I have your attention please. This is a Manhattan-bound E train, making express stops. The next stop is Jamaica Station. Passengers who have to transfer to the AirTrain, this is your stop, and you have to leave the train here. Please remember that you don't have to tap your card again for that transfer. Your fare covers the whole trip. Because of construction work this weekend, we are running express from Jamaica to Queens Plaza. That means we can't stop at Sutphin Boulevard, and we can't stop at Kew Gardens. If you need those stations, you have to get off here and take the local train on the other platform. And one more reminder. You are not allowed to hold the doors. Holding the doors delays the train for everybody behind you. Stand clear of the closing doors, please.

Welcome back. Today, three things nobody tells you about the New York subway, and I learned all three of them the hard way. Number one. The direction matters more than the line. Every tourist checks the letter. Is it the E, is it the F, is it the six. Nobody checks whether the platform says uptown or downtown. I once rode forty blocks the wrong way, and I have to tell you, the train does not stop and turn around for you. You have to go up the stairs, cross the street, and come back down on the other side. Number two. You don't have to buy anything. Not a card, not a ticket, not an app. You just tap your credit card at the turnstile and you walk through. People still stand in line at the machine for fifteen minutes, and they don't have to. Number three. The express train is not always faster for you. It is faster for the train. If your station is a local station, the express will take you straight past it, and now you have to come back. So before you get excited about the express, you have to check whether it actually stops where you are going. And one last thing you can't do, and everybody does it anyway. Do not hold the doors. Just don't. The next train is four minutes away.

paras: The check-in kiosk in Guarulhos takes her four minutes. There is a line of forty people at the counter, and a line of two at the machines, and Lucimara has learned which line to stand in. She does not have to talk to anybody. She scans her passport, she prints her tag, and she is done. Her connecting flight leaves Atlanta at seven forty, and the departure gate is on the far side of the terminal, so she walks fast and she does not stop for coffee.

Eleven hours later she is standing in front of a wall of signs at Kennedy, and the signs are all talking to her at once. Her rideshare app says the driver is four minutes away, then five, then six. He calls, and he speaks so fast that she catches one word in three. He says he is downstairs. He says he can't wait. Later she will learn what she is learning now: at that airport, rideshare cars are not allowed to stop at the curbside on the arrivals level. He has to come up to Level 2, and she has to walk up, and neither of them knows this, so he cancels and she takes the shuttle to the station instead. It costs her eleven dollars and it saves her sixty.

At the station there is a man in a booth, and this is where the trip turns. She has a large suitcase and the turnstile is too narrow for it. "You have to use the wide gate," he says, "and you have to tap there too." She asks whether she has to buy a MetroCard. She doesn't. She can just tap her credit card, the same one that is already in her hand, and she can feel the fifteen minutes she is not going to spend in line at the machine. Then she asks the question that saves her: which platform does she have to be on, uptown or downtown?

It is the question she almost did not ask, and it is the only one that mattered. Both platforms say E. Both trains are the same color, on the same line, with the same letter on the front. One of them goes to Midtown, where her hotel is. The other one goes forty blocks in the opposite direction, and it is an express train, which means it would not stop again for a long, long time. She takes the downtown platform. Sixty-two minutes after the plane touched the ground, she is standing at the front desk of her hotel in Midtown, and she has not spoken a single word of Portuguese since Guarulhos.

gist.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: New York is a difficult city, and tourists should take a taxi instead of the subway.
Getting across a city is not about knowing the map. It is about asking for the rule before you need it.
Rideshare apps do not work well at airports.

items: She had to talk to somebody at the counter to check in.
"She does not have to talk to anybody. She scans her passport, she prints her tag, and she is done." -- she used the check-in kiosk on purpose.
She was not allowed to buy a paper ticket for the subway.
Careful -- she simply did not NEED one: "She asks whether she has to buy a MetroCard. She doesn't." Doesn't have to (optional) is not can't (forbidden).
The rideshare driver could not pick her up on the arrivals level.
"rideshare cars are not allowed to stop at the curbside on the arrivals level. He has to come up to Level 2."
Her suitcase did not fit through the turnstile, so she had to use the wide gate.
"the turnstile is too narrow for it. 'You have to use the wide gate,' he says, 'and you have to tap there too.'"
Both platforms had trains with the same letter, going in opposite directions.
"Both platforms say E... One of them goes to Midtown... The other one goes forty blocks in the opposite direction." That is why the question about the platform was the only one that mattered.

items.situation: You are at the booth with a large suitcase, and the turnstile is too narrow. Ask the agent what you have to do.
tips: Do I have to use another gate? This suitcase doesn't fit through the turnstile.
Closed question = short answer. Ask for the rule, not for a story.

items.situation: Two platforms, same letter, same color. You have four seconds before the train leaves. Ask.
tips: Which platform do I have to be on -- uptown or downtown?
This is the question that saves the whole trip. Do not guess it.

items.situation: The agent tells you the E is an express train. Find out if that is good news or bad news for you.
tips: Does the express train stop at 42nd Street, or do I have to take the local one?
Express is only faster if it stops where you are going.

items.situation: Your rideshare driver is waiting on Level 1, and the police are moving the cars along. Tell him the rule.
tips: You can't wait down there -- you have to come up to Level 2. Pickup is only allowed at the curbside upstairs.
Here you are the one who knows the rule. Say it with can't and have to.

items.situation: A tourist behind you is standing in line at the machine, about to buy a ticket she does not need. Free her -- careful with this one.
tips: You don't have to buy a ticket -- you can just tap your credit card at the turnstile.
Trap: if you say 'you can't buy a ticket', you just forbade her. Don't have to = optional. Can't = forbidden.

items.situation: The agent has just given you the line, the platform and the transfer, fast. Close the conversation the safe way.
tips: Let me check I understood: the E train, downtown platform, no transfer. Is that right?
Confirm before you walk away. One sentence, and you never get it wrong.
